front: Composer: Austrian; died at age 31; wrote over 600 lieder; works catalogued with 'D.' numbers by Otto Deutsch
Composer: wrote 8 impromptus in two sets and 6 Moments Musicaux, expanding a form invented by Jan Václav Vořišek
Composer: his Octet in F was commissioned by Ferdinand Troyer and adds a second violin to Beethoven's Septet instrumentation
Song Cycle: 24 songs on Wilhelm Müller poems; ends with the narrator meeting a hurdy-gurdy player, whose instrument is depicted by repeated open fifths
work: Song Cycles (Winterreise, Die schöne Müllerin, Schwanengesang)

front: Song Cycle: the linden tree song features rustling E-major triplets in the accompaniment as the narrator recalls resting under it
work: Song Cycles (Winterreise, Die schöne Müllerin, Schwanengesang)

front: Song Cycle: the miller's apprentice drowns himself in the brook, which turns out to be the villain; the cycle ends with the brook's E-major lullaby
work: Song Cycles (Winterreise, Die schöne Müllerin, Schwanengesang)

front: Song Cycle: posthumously published 14 songs including 'Der Doppelgänger' (B–A#–D–C# ostinato, setting Heine) and 'Die Taubenpost' (often Schubert's last lied)
work: Song Cycles (Winterreise, Die schöne Müllerin, Schwanengesang)

front: Lied: piano right hand plays repeated G octave triplets for the galloping horse; four vocal personas (narrator, father, boy, Erlking); boy is dead at the end
Lied: sets a Goethe poem; the boy's pitch rises with each appearance to show mounting fear; last words are 'war tot'
Symphony: only two completed movements and a fragmentary scherzo; Brian Newbould proposed using the Rosamunde B minor entr'acte as a finale
Symphony: opens with cellos and basses on B–C#–D–B; score recovered from the composer's brother
Symphony: praised by Schumann for 'heavenly length'; revived by Mendelssohn; opens with two unison French horns on C–D–E–A–B–C
Symphony: finale's development quotes the 'Ode to Joy' in E-flat; violinists at London premiere reportedly collapsed laughing at the ricochet ostinato
Work: piano quintet that replaces the second violin with a double bass; fourth movement is variations on 'Die Forelle'; commissioned by Sylvester Paumgartner
String Quartet: second movement is variations on the earlier lied 'Der Tod und das Mädchen'; finale is a D minor Presto tarantella
back: String Quartet No. 14, 'Death and the Maiden' (Schubert)
work: String Quartet No. 14 in D minor, 'Death and the Maiden'

front: String Quartet: preceded by the 'Rosamunde' Quartet (No. 13), which reuses incidental music to a Helmina von Chézy play
back: String Quartet No. 14, 'Death and the Maiden' (Schubert)
work: String Quartet No. 14 in D minor, 'Death and the Maiden'

front: String Quartet: Schubert's only string quintet; adds a second cello; ends with a D-flat grace note resolving to C; second movement is his finest adagio
Work: C major piano fantasy; Schubert said 'the devil may play it'; based on the lied 'Der Wanderer' ('The sun seems so cold to me here'); later orchestrated by Liszt
Sonata: the final B-flat major piano sonata (D. 960) has a mysterious bass G-flat trill eight measures in, called the 'Trill of Doom' by Alex Ross
back: Piano Sonata No. 21 in B-flat major (Schubert)

front: Sonata: last three sonatas in C minor, A major, and B-flat major (D. 958–960) are so thematically knit they are often performed as a trilogy
